result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sells honey in mason jars, and every Saturday she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they mourn when one of their sisters dies. I buy a jar each week, not because I need that much honey, but because I need to hear someone talk about loving something that could hurt them.

My kitchen cabinet is full of amber jars now, catching light like captured sunset. Sometimes I open them just to smell summer – that thick sweetness that speaks of flowers I'll never see, fields I'll never walk through. The bees traveled miles to make this. The old woman's crooked fingers labeled each jar with the month and year, as if time could be preserved in sweetness.

I've been thinking about preservation lately. How we're all trying to save something. My neighbor presses flowers between dictionary pages. She showed me once – violet pansies flattened between "melancholy" and "memory." She says she's making bookmarks for her grandchildren, but they never visit. The flowers accumulate like silent words.

There's a certain kind of loneliness that comes from keeping things. Museum loneliness. Archive loneliness. The careful distance between the thing preserved and the life it once held. I think about this while spreading honey on toast, wondering if the bees remember the flowers, if sweetness is its own kind of memory.

My mother called yesterday. She's cleaning out the attic, finding boxes of my childhood drawings. "Should I keep them?" she asked, and I could hear the weight of thirty years in her voice. The weight of refrigerator art and handprint turkeys, crayon suns with stick-figure families beneath them. "I don't know," I told her, because I don't know if love means holding on or letting go.

The truth is, I'm afraid of both. Afraid of empty hands and afraid of hands too full to hold anything new. The honey jars multiply. The dictionary grows thick with pressed flowers. My mother's attic holds the child I was, suspended in construction paper and glitter glue.

But here's what the bee woman doesn't say, what I've learned from watching her weathered hands move among the jars: the bees don't make honey for us. They make it for winter, for survival, for reasons that have nothing to do with our need to sweeten things. We're just lucky observers, careful thieves of their industry.

Maybe that's what all preservation is – a kind of beautiful theft. We take the flower from the field, the moment from time's stream, the honey from the hive. We label it, date it, put it behind glass or between pages. We say we're saving it, but really we're just trying to save ourselves from the passing of things.

Last week, the bee woman wasn't at her usual stall. Her nephew was there instead, selling the same honey in the same mason jars. "She's with her bees," he said, which could mean anything. Could mean tending them. Could mean becoming earth that feeds the flowers that feed the bees that make the honey that fills the jars that line my cabinet like a prayer to permanence.

I bought three jars from him. He didn't tell me any stories.

Walking home, I passed the cemetery where stones mark attempts at permanent memory. Names and dates carved deep, but even stone erodes. Even granite forgets. There were fresh flowers on some graves, plastic flowers on others. The plastic ones last longer but mean less, or maybe they mean more – the peculiar devotion of choosing what endures over what's real.

I want to call my mother back. Tell her to throw it all away. Tell her to keep every scrap. Tell her that love isn't measured in what we keep but in what we're willing to carry. But I don't call. Instead, I open a jar from last summer, spread honey on warm bread, and taste time itself – the mornings the bees flew out, the afternoons they returned heavy with nectar, the nights they fanned their wings to thicken sweetness.

Tomorrow is Saturday. If the bee woman is back, I'll buy another jar. If she isn't, I'll buy one anyway. Her nephew doesn't need to know that my cabinet is full, that I've started giving jars away to strangers, pressing honey into their hands like sharing a secret about the world.

Here's the secret: everything we love will leave us, is leaving us, has already left. The bees die after they sting, defending sweetness. The flowers pressed in dictionaries were dead the moment they were picked. My mother's attic holds a child who doesn't exist anymore.

But here's the other secret: the honey remains sweet. The pressed flowers still hold their color. The child in those drawings was real, is real, will always be real in the way that past things are real – not present but persistent, gone but glowing, like light from dead stars still reaching us, still bright, still guiding us home.

The honey catches morning light through my window. Amber philosophy, golden archaeology. Each jar a small world, complete and contained.
